q: Let $[\cdot]$ be the greatest integer function. If $\alpha=\int_{0}^{64}\left(x^{1 / 3}-\left[x^{1 / 3}\right]\right) \mathrm{d} x$, then $\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\alpha \pi}\left(\frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta}\right) \mathrm{d} \theta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
question: Let $[\cdot]$ be the greatest integer function. If $\alpha=\int_{0}^{64}\left(x^{1 / 3}-\left[x^{1 / 3}\right]\right) \mathrm{d} x$, then $\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\alpha \pi}\left(\frac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin ^{6} \theta+\cos ^{6} \theta}\right) \mathrm{d} \theta$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
$\because \int_{0}^{64} x^{\frac{1}{3}} \, dx = \dfrac{3}{4} \left[ x^{\frac{4}{3}} \right]_{0}^{64} = 192$ &
$\int_{0}^{64} [x^{1/3}] \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} [x^{1/3}] \, dx + \int_{1}^{8} [x^{1/3}] \, dx + \int_{8}^{27} [x^{1/3}] \, dx + \int_{27}^{64} [x^{1/3}] \, dx = 156$
So $\alpha = 192 - 156 = 36$
Now $E = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{36\pi} \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$
$= \dfrac{36}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$
$\Rightarrow E = \dfrac{36 \cdot 2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$
Let $J = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$ ....(1)
Applying King
$J = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$ .....(2)
Now $2J = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \dfrac{1}{\sin^6\theta + \cos^6\theta} \, d\theta$ (add (1) & (2))
$= \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \dfrac{\sec^6\theta}{\tan^6\theta + 1} \, d\theta$
$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(1 + \lambda^2)}{\lambda^4 - \lambda^2 + 1} \, d\lambda$
$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}}{\lambda^2 - 1 + \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}} \, d\lambda$
$= \pi$
$\Rightarrow J = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow E = \dfrac{36 \cdot 2}{\pi} \times J = 36$

limn→∞n+1k-1nk+1(nk+1)+(nk+2)+…+nk+n=33

=33limn→∞1nk+11k+2k+…+nk=33limn→∞∑k=1n1nknk

=33∫01xkdx=33k+1

Now given, LHS=RHS

⇒k+12=33·1k+1

⇒2k+1k+1=66

⇒k-52k+13=0

⇒k=5 or -132

So, integral value of k=5

q: If $\displaystyle\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/4}\left(\cot\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\cot\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right)dx = \alpha\log_e(\sqrt{3}-1)$, then $9\alpha^2$ is equal to ________.
question: If $\displaystyle\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/4}\left(\cot\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\cot\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right)dx = \alpha\log_e(\sqrt{3}-1)$, then $9\alpha^2$ is equal to ________.
solution:
Given integral is $I = \displaystyle\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/4}\left(\cot\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\cot\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right)dx$

Using the identity $\cot A \cot B + 1 = \dfrac{\cos(A-B)}{\sin A \sin B}$, the integrand simplifies to:

$f(x) = \dfrac{\cos\left(\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) - \left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)}{\sin\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\sin\left(x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)}$

$f(x) = \dfrac{\cos\left(-\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)}{\sin^2 x - \sin^2\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)}$

$f(x) = \dfrac{-1/2}{\sin^2 x - 3/4} = \dfrac{2}{3 - 4\sin^2 x}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $\cos^2 x$:

$f(x) = \dfrac{2\sec^2 x}{3\sec^2 x - 4\tan^2 x} = \dfrac{2\sec^2 x}{3(1+\tan^2 x) - 4\tan^2 x} = \dfrac{2\sec^2 x}{3 - \tan^2 x}$

The integral becomes:

$I = \displaystyle\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/4} \dfrac{2\sec^2 x}{3 - \tan^2 x} dx$

Substituting $\tan x = t \Rightarrow \sec^2 x dx = dt$.

When $x = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$, $t = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$. When $x = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$, $t = 1$.

$I = \displaystyle\int_{1/\sqrt{3}}^{1} \dfrac{2}{3 - t^2} dt$

Using the standard integral $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{a^2 - x^2} dx = \dfrac{1}{2a}\ln\left|\dfrac{a+x}{a-x}\right|$:

$I = 2 \left[ \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \ln\left|\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+t}{\sqrt{3}-t}\right| \right]_{1/\sqrt{3}}^{1}$

$I = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \left( \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right) - \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}{\sqrt{3}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}\right) \right)$

$I = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \left( \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right) - \ln\left(\dfrac{3+1}{3-1}\right) \right)$

$I = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \left( \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right) - \ln 2 \right) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \ln\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)}\right)$

Rationalizing the term inside the logarithm by multiplying the numerator and the denominator by $(\sqrt{3}-1)$:

$\dfrac{\sqrt{3}+1}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3}+1)(\sqrt{3}-1)}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)^2} = \dfrac{3-1}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2} = (\sqrt{3}-1)^{-2}$

Thus,

$I = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \ln\left((\sqrt{3}-1)^{-2}\right) = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \ln(\sqrt{3}-1)$

Comparing this with $I = \alpha\log_e(\sqrt{3}-1)$, we get:

$\alpha = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$

Therefore, $9\alpha^2 = 9 \left(-\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = 9 \times \dfrac{4}{3} = 12$.

Answer: $12$

q: Let for $f(x)=7 \tan ^8 x+7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^4 x-3 \tan ^2 x, \quad \mathrm{I}_1=\int_0^{\pi / 4} f(x) \mathrm{d} x$ and $\mathrm{I}_2=\int_0^{\pi / 4} x f(x) \mathrm{d} x$. Then $7 \mathrm{I}_1+12 \mathrm{I}_2$ is equal to :
question: Let for $f(x)=7 \tan ^8 x+7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^4 x-3 \tan ^2 x, \quad \mathrm{I}_1=\int_0^{\pi / 4} f(x) \mathrm{d} x$ and $\mathrm{I}_2=\int_0^{\pi / 4} x f(x) \mathrm{d} x$. Then $7 \mathrm{I}_1+12 \mathrm{I}_2$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} f(x) & =7 \tan ^8 x+7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^4 x-3 \tan ^2 x \\ & =7 \tan ^6 x\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)-3 \tan ^2 x\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \\ & =\left(7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^2 x\right)\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \\ & =\left(7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x \\ l_1= & \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} f(x) d x=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x d x \\ & =\left.\left(\frac{7 \tan ^7 x}{7}-\frac{3 \tan ^3 x}{3}\right)\right|_0 ^{\frac{\pi}{4}}=1-1=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
I_2= & \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} x f(x) d x=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} x\left(7 \tan ^6 x-3 \tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x d x \\
& =\left.x\left(\tan ^7 x-\tan ^3 x\right)\right|_0 ^{\frac{\pi}{4}}-\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} 1 \cdot\left(\tan ^7 x-\tan ^3 x\right) d x \\
& =0-\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan ^3 x\left(\tan ^2 x-1\right)\left(\tan ^2 x+1\right) d x \\
& =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\tan ^3 x-\tan ^5 x\right) \sec ^2 x d x=\frac{\tan ^4 x}{4}-\left.\frac{\tan ^6 x}{6}\right|_0 ^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \\
& =\frac{1}{12}
\end{aligned}$

Hence $7 I_1+12 I_2=1$

q: Let $\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2} (|\sin x| + [x \sin x])\,dx = 2(3 - \cos 2) + \beta$, where $[\cdot]$ is the greatest integer function. Then $\beta \sin\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)$ equals:
question: Let $\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2} (|\sin x| + [x \sin x])\,dx = 2(3 - \cos 2) + \beta$, where $[\cdot]$ is the greatest integer function. Then $\beta \sin\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)$ equals:
solution:
Let $I = \displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2} \left(|\sin x| + [x \sin x]\right) dx = I_1 + I_2$, where
$I_1 = \displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2} |\sin x|\, dx$ and $I_2 = \displaystyle\int_{-2}^{2} [x \sin x]\, dx$.

Evaluating $I_1$:
Since $|\sin x|$ is even, $I_1 = 2\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2} |\sin x|\, dx$.
For $x \in [0, 2]$, $2 < \pi$, so $\sin x \geq 0$ and $|\sin x| = \sin x$.
$I_1 = 2\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2} \sin x\, dx = 2\left[-\cos x\right]_0^2 = 2(1 - \cos 2) = 2 - 2\cos 2$

Evaluating $I_2$:
Let $g(x) = x\sin x$. Then $g(-x) = (-x)\sin(-x) = x\sin x = g(x)$, so $g(x)$ is even, and hence $[x\sin x]$ is also even.
$I_2 = 2\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2} [x\sin x]\, dx$

Behaviour of $y = x\sin x$ on $[0, 2]$:
At $x = 0$, $y = 0$. At $x = 2$, $y = 2\sin 2 \approx 1.818$.
$y' = \sin x + x\cos x > 0$ on $[0, 2]$, so $y$ is strictly increasing on this interval.

Therefore, $x\sin x$ takes each value in $[0, 1.818]$ exactly once, and crosses $1$ at some unique $\alpha \in (0, 2)$ with $\alpha\sin\alpha = 1$.

For $x \in [0, \alpha)$: $0 \leq x\sin x < 1 \Rightarrow [x\sin x] = 0$
For $x \in [\alpha, 2]$: $1 \leq x\sin x < 2 \Rightarrow [x\sin x] = 1$

$I_2 = 2\left(\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\alpha} 0\, dx + \displaystyle\int_{\alpha}^{2} 1\, dx\right) = 2(2 - \alpha) = 4 - 2\alpha$

Combining:
$I = I_1 + I_2 = (2 - 2\cos 2) + (4 - 2\alpha) = 6 - 2\cos 2 - 2\alpha$

Given $I = 2(3 - \cos 2) + \beta = 6 - 2\cos 2 + \beta$, so:
$6 - 2\cos 2 - 2\alpha = 6 - 2\cos 2 + \beta \Rightarrow \beta = -2\alpha$

Now:
$\beta \sin\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) = -2\alpha \sin\left(\dfrac{-2\alpha}{2}\right) = -2\alpha \sin(-\alpha) = 2\alpha\sin\alpha$

Since $\alpha\sin\alpha = 1$:
$\beta \sin\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ 2$.

question: If [ ] denotes the greatest integer function, then the integral $\int_0^\pi[\cos x d x$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_0^\pi[\cos x] d x$
$$
\mathrm{I}=\int_0^\pi[\cos (\pi-x)] d x=\int_0^\pi[-\cos x] d x
$$
On adding (1) and (2), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&2 \mathrm{I}=\int_0^\pi[\cos x] d x+\int_0^\pi[-\cos x] d x \\
&2 \mathrm{I}=\int_0^\pi[\cos x]+[-\cos x] d x \\
&2 \mathrm{I} \\
&=\int_0^\pi-1 d x \quad(\because[x]+[-x]=-1 \text { if } x \notin Z) \\
&2 \mathrm{I}=-\left.x\right|_0 ^\pi=-\pi \\
&\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{-\pi}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
